To journey; to ride to a distant place in the same country; as, a man travels for his health; he is traveling to Virginia. A man traveled from London to Edinburgh in five days.

To go to a distant country, or to visit foreign states or kingdoms, either by sea or land. It is customary for men of rank and property to travel for improvement. Englishmen travel to France and Italy. Some men travel for pleasure or curiosity; others travel to extend their knowledge of natural history.
To pass; to go; to move. News travels with rapidity. Time travels in divers paces with divers persons.
traveled: Gained or made by travel; as traveled observations.
traveler: One who travels in any way. Job 31:32.
In ships, an iron thimble or thimbles with a rope spliced round them, forming a kind of tail or a species of grommet.
traveling: Walking; going; making a journey. Matthew 25:14. 2 .a. Incurred by travel; as traveling expenses.
Paid for travel; as traveling fees.
traversable: That may be traversed or denied; as a traversable allegation.
traverse: Athwart; crosswise. The ridges of the field lay traverse.
Through crosswise. He traverse . The whole battalion views their order due.
Lying across; being in a direction across something else; as paths cut with traverse trenches. Oak--may be trusted in traverse work for summers.
Any thing laid or built across. There is a traverse placed in the loft where she sitteth.
Something that thwarts, crosses or obstructs; a cross accident. He is satisfied he should have succeeded, had it not been for unlucky traverses not in his power.
traverse-board: In a ship, a small board to be hung in the steerage, and bored full of holes upon lines, showing the points of compass upon it. By moving a peg on this, the steersman keeps an account of the number of glasses a ship is steered on any point.
traverse-table: In navigation, a table of difference of latitude and departure.
traversing: Crossing; passing over; thwarting; turning; denying.
travestied: Disguised by dress; turned into ridicule.
travestin: A kind of white spongy stone found in Italy.
travesty: Having an unusual dress; disguised by dress so as to be ridiculous. It is applied to a book or composition translated in a manner to make it burlesque.
A parody; a burlesque translation of a work. Travesty may be intended to ridicule absurdity, or to convert a grave performance into a humorous one.
To translate into such language as to render ridiculous or ludicrous. G. Battista Lalli travestied Virgil, or turned him into Italian burlesque verse.
tray: A small trough or wooden vessel, sometimes scooped out of a piece of timber and made hollow, used for making bread in, chopping meat and other domestic purposes.
treacherous: trech’erous. Violating allegiance of faith pledged; faithless; traitorous to the state or sovereign; perfidious in private life; betraying a trust. A man may be treacherous to his country, or treacherous to his friend, by violating his engagements or his faith pledged.
treacherously: trech’erously. By violating allegiance or faith pledged; by betraying a trust; faithlessly; perfidiously; as, to surrender a fort to an enemy treacherously; to disclose a secret treacherously. You treacherously practic’d to undo me.
treacherousness: trech’erousness. Breach of allegiance or of faith; faithlessness; perfidiousness.
treachery: trech’ery. Violation of allegiance or of faith and confidence. The man who betrays his country in any manner, violates his allegiance, and is guilty of treachery. This is treason. The man who violates his faith pledged to his friend, or betrays a trust in which a promise of fidelity is implied, is guilty of treachery. The disclosure of a secret committed to one in confidence, is treachery. This is perfidy.
treacle: The spume of sugar in sugar refineries. Treacle is obtained in refining sugar; molasses is the drainings of crude sugar. Treacle however is often used for molasses.
A saccharine fluid, consisting of the inspissated juices or decoctions of certain vegetables, as the sap of the birch, sycamore.
A medicinal compound of various’ ingredients. [See Theriaca.; .
treacle-mustard: A plant of the genus Thlaspi, whose seeds are used in the theriaca; Mithridate mustard.
treacle-water: A compound cordial, distilled with a spiritous menstruum from any cordial and sudorific drugs and herbs, with a mixture of Venice treacle.
tread: To set the foot. Where’er you tread, the blushing flow’rs shall rise. Fools rush in where angels fear to tread.
To walk or go. Every place whereon the soles of your feet shall tread, shall be yours. Deuteronomy 11:24.
To walk with form or state. Ye that stately tread, or lowly creep.
To copulate, as fowls. To tread or tread on, to trample; to set the foot on in contempt. Thou shalt tread upon their high places. Deuteronomy 33:29.
treading: tred’ing. Stepping; pressing with the foot; walking on.
treadle: The part of a loom or other machine which is moved by the tread or foot.
The albuminous cords which unite the yolk of the egg to the white.
treddle: The part of a loom or other machine which is moved by the tread or foot.
The albuminous cords which unite the yolk of the egg to the white.
treason: tree’zn. Treason is the highest crime of a civil nature of which a man can be guilty. Its signification is different in different countries. In general, it is the offense of attempting to overthrow the government of the state to which the offender owes allegiance, or of betraying the state into the hands of a foreign power. In monarchies, the killing of the king, or an attempt to take his life, is treason. In England, to imagine or compass the death of the king, or of the prince, or of the queen consort, or of the heir apparent of the crown, is high treason; as are many other offenses created by statute. In the United States, treason is confined to the actual levying of war against the United States, or in adhering to their enemies, giving them aid and comfort. Treason in Great Britain, is of two kinds, high treason and petit treason. High treason is a crime that immediately affects the
treasonable: tree’znable. Pertaining to treason; consisting of treason; involving the crime of treason, or partaking of its guilt. Most men’s heads had been intoxicated with imaginations of plots and treasonable practices.
treasonous: for treasonable, is not in use.
treasure: Wealth accumulated; particularly, a stock or store of money in reserve. Henry VII. was frugal and penurious, and collected a great treasure of gold and silver.
A great quantity of any thing collected for future use. We have treasures in the field, of wheat and of barley, and of oil and of honey. Jeremiah 41:8.
Something very much valued. Psalm 135:4.£ Ye shall be a peculiar treasure to me. Exodus 19:5.
Great abundance. In whom are hid all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge. Colossians 2:3.

trezh’ur. To hoard; to collect and reposit, either money or other things, for future use; to lay up; as, to treasure gold and silver; usually with up. Sinners are said to treasure up wrath against the day of wrath. Romans 2:5.
treasure-city: trezh’ur-city. A city for stores and magazines. Exodus 1:11.
treasured: trezh’ured. Hoarded; laid up for future use.
treasure-house: trezh’ur-house. A house or building where treasures and stores are kept.
treasurer: trezh’urer. One who has the care of a treasure or treasury; an officer who receives the public money arising from taxes and duties or other sources of revenue, takes charge of the same, and disburses it upon orders drawn by the proper authority. Incorporated companies and private societies have also their treasurers. In England, the lord high treasurer is the principal officer of the crown, under whose charge is all the national revenue. The treasurer of the household, in the absence of the lord-steward, has power with the controller and other officers of the Green-cloth, and the steward of the Marshalsea, to hear and determine treasons, felonies and other crimes committed within the king’s palace. There is also the treasurer of the navy, and the treasurers of the county.
treasuress: trezh’uress. A female who has charge of a treasure.
treasure-trove: trezh’ur-trove. Any money, bullion and the like, found in the earth, the owner of which is not known.
treasury: trezh’ury. A place or building in which stores of wealth are reposited; particularly, a place where the public revenues are deposited and kept, and where money is disbursed to defray the expenses of government.
A building appropriated for keeping public money. John 8:20.
The officer or officers of the treasury department.
treat: To handle; to manage; to use. Subjects are usually faithful or treacherous, according as they are well or ill treated. To treat prisoners ill, is the characteristic of barbarians. Let the wife of your bosom be kindly treated.
To handle in a particular manner, in writing or speaking; as, to treat a subject diffusely.
To entertain without expense to the guest.
To negotiate; to settle; as, to treat a peace.
To manage in the application of remedies; as, to treat a disease or a patient.
treatable: Moderate; not violent. The heats or the colds of seasons are less treatable than with us.
treated: Handled; managed; used; discoursed on; entertained.
treater: One that treats; one that handles or discourses on; one that entertains.
treating: Handling; managing; using; discoursing on; entertaining.
treatise: A tract; a written composition on a particular subject, in which the principles of it are discussed or explained. A treatise is of an indefinite length; but it implies more form and method than an essay, and less fullness or copiousness than a system.
treatment: Management; manipulation; manner of mixing or combining, of decomposing and the like; as the treatment of substances in chimical experiments.
Usage; manner of using; good of bad behavior towards. Accept such treatment as a swain affords.
Manner of applying remedies to cure; mode or course pursued to check and destroy; as the treatment of a disease.
Manner of applying remedies to; as the treatment of a patient .
treaty: Negotiation; act of treating for the adjustment of differences, or for forming an agreement; as, a treaty is on the carpet. He cast by treaty and by trains . Her to persuade.

An agreement, league or contract between two or more nations or sovereigns, formally signed by commissioners — properly authorized, and solemnly ratified by the several sovereigns or the supreme power of each state. Treaties are of various kinds, as treaties for regulating commercial intercourse, treaties of alliance, offensive and defensive, treaties for hiring troops, treaties of peace.
treaty-making: The treaty-making power is lodged in the executive government. In monarchies, it is vested in the king or emperor; in the United States of America, it is vested in the president, by and with the consent of the senate.
treble: Threefold; triple; as a lofty tower with treble walls.
In music, acute; sharp; as a treble sound.
That plays the highest part or most acute sounds; that plays the treble; as a treble violin.
trib’l. In music, the part of a symphony whose sounds are highest or most acute. This is divided into first or highest treble, and second or base treble.
trib’l. To make thrice as much; to make threefold. Compound interest soon trebles a debt.
trebleness: trib’Iness. The state of being treble; as the trebleness of tones.
trebly: trib’ly. In a threefold number or quantity; as a good deed trebly recompensed.
tree: The general name of the largest of the vegetable kind, consisting of a firm woody stem springing from woody roots, and spreading above into branches which terminate in leaves. A tree differs from a shrub principally in size, many species of trees growing to the highth of fifty or sixty feet, and some species to seventy or eighty, and a few, particularly the pine, to a much greater highth. Trees are of various kinds; as nuciferous, or nut-bearing trees; bacciferous, or berry-bearing; coniferous, or cone-bearingSome are forest-trees, and useful for timber or fuel; others are fruit trees, and cultivated in gardens and orchards; others are used chiefly for shade and ornament.
Something resembling a tree, consisting of a stem or stalk and branches; as a genealogical tree.
In ship-building, pieces of timber are called chess-trees, cross- trees, roof-trees, tressel-trees.
In Scripture, a cross. --Jesus, whom they slew and hanged on a tree. Acts 10:39.
tree-frog: A species of frog, the Rana arborea, found on trees and shrubs; called by the older writers, Ranunculus viridis.
tree-germander: A plant of the genus Teucrium.
tree-louse: An insect of the genus Aphis.
tree-nail: A long wooden pin, used in fastening the planks of a ship to the timbers.
tree-of-life: An evergreen tree of the genus Thuja.
tree-toad: A small species of toad in N. America, found on trees. This animal croaks chiefly in the evening and after a rain.
trefoil: The common name for many plants of the genus Trifolium; also, in agriculture, a name of the medicago tupulina, a plant resembling clover, with yellow flowers, much cultivated for hay and fodder. Haw eouI
treillage: trel’lage. In gardening, a sort of rail-work, consisting of light posts and rails for supporting espaliers, and sometimes for wall trees.
trellis: In gardening, a structure or frame of cross-barred work, or lattice work, used like the treillage for supporting plants.
tremble: To shake involuntarily, as with fear, cold or weakness; to quake; to quiver; to shiver; to shudder. Frighted Turnus trembled as he spoke.
To shake; to quiver; to totter. Sinai’s gray top shall tremble.
To quaver; to shake, as sound; as when we say, the voice trembles.
tremblement: In French music, a trill or shake.
trembling: Shaking, as with fear, cold or weakness; quaking; shivering.
tremblingly: So as to shake; with shivering or quaking. Tremblingly she stood.
tremendous: Such as may excite fear or terror; terrible; dreadful. Hence,
Violent; such as may astonish by its force and violence; as a tremendous wind; a tremendous shower; a tremendous shock or fall; a tremendous noise.
tremendously: In a manner to terrify or astonish; with great violence.
tremendousness: The state or quality of being tremendous, terrible or violent.
tremolite: A mineral, so called from Tremola, a valley in the Alps, where it was discovered. It is classed by Hauy with hornblend or amphibole, and called amphibole grammatite. It is of three kinds, asbestos, common, and glassy tremolite; all of a fibrous or radiated structure, and of a pearly color. Tremolite is a subspecies of straight edged augite.
tremor: An involuntary trembling; a shivering or shaking; a quivering or vibratory motion; as the tremor of a person who is weak, infirm or old. He fell into a universal tremor.
tremulous: Trembling; affected with fear or timidity; as a trembling christian.
Shaking; shivering; quivering; as a tremulous limb; a tremulous motion of the hand or the lips; the tremulous leaf of the poplar.
tremulousness: The state of trembling or quivering; as the tremulousness of an aspen leaf.
trench: To cut or dig, as a ditch, a channel for water, or a long hollow in the earth. We trench land for draining.
To fortify by cutting a ditch and raising a rampart or breast-work of earth thrown out of the ditch.
To furrow; to form with deep furrows by plowing.
trenched: Cut into long hollows or ditches; furrowed deep.
trencher: A wooden plate. Trenchers were in use among the common people of New England till the revolution.
Food; pleasures of the table. It would be no ordinary declension that would bring some men to place their summum bonum upon their trenchers.
trencher-ely: One that haunts the tables of others; a parasite.
trencher-friend: One who frequents the tables of others; a spunger.
trench-plow: A kind of plow for opening land to a greater depth than that of common furrows.
trench-plowing: The practice or operation of plowing with deep furrows, for the purpose of loosening the land to a greater depth than usual.
trend: To run; to stretch; to tend; to have a particular direction; as, the shore of the sea trends to the southwest.
That part of the stock of an anchor from which the size is taken.
In rural economy, to free wool from its filth.
trender: One whose business is to free wool from its filth.
trending: The operation of freeing wool from filth of various kinds.
trendle: Any thing round used in turning or rolling; a little wheel.
trental: An office for the dead in the Romish service, consisting of thirty masses rehearsed for thirty days successively after the party’s death.
trentals: An office for the dead in the Romish service, consisting of thirty masses rehearsed for thirty days successively after the party’s death.
trepan: In surgery, a circular saw for perforating the skull. It resembles a wimble.

To perforate the skull and take out a piece; a surgical operation for relieving the brain from pressure or irritation. Trepan, a snare, and trepan, to ensnare, are from trap, and written trepan, which see.
trepanning: Perforating the skull with a trepan.
The operation of making an opening in the skull, for relieving the brain from compression or irritation.
trephine: An instrument for trepanning, more modern than the trepan. It is a circular or cylindrical saw, with a handle like that of a gimblet, and a little sharp perforator, called the center-pin.
To perforate with a trephine; to trepan.
trepidation: An involuntary trembling; a quaking or quivering, particularly from fear or terror; hence, a state of terror. The men were in great trepidation.
A trembling of the limbs, as in paralytic affections.
In the old astronomy, a libration of the eighth sphere, or a motion which the Ptolemaic system ascribes to the firmament, to account for the changes and motion of the axis of the world.
trespass: Literally, to pass beyond; hence primarily, to pass over the boundary line of another’s land; to enter unlawfully upon the land of another. A man may trespass by walking over the ground of another, and the law gives a remedy for damages sustained. IAD .

To commit any offense or to do any act that injures or annoys another; to violate any rule of rectitude to the injury of another. If any man shall trespass against his neighbor, and an oath be laid upon him-- 7 Kings 8:31. See Luke 17:3, 44 .

In a moral sense, to transgress voluntarily any divine law or command; to violate any known rule of duty. In the time of his disease did he trespass yet more. 2 Chronicles 28:22. We have trespassed against our God.FEzra 10:2.
To intrude; to go too far; to put to inconvenience by demand or importunity; as, to trespass upon the time or patience of another.

In law, violation of another’s rights, not amounting to treason, felony, or misprision of either. Thus to enter another’s close, is a trespass; to attack his person is a trespass. When violence accompanies the act, it is called a trespass vi et armis.
trespasser: One who commits a trespass; one who enters upon another’s land or violates his rights.
A transgressor of the moral law; an offender; a sinner.
trespassing: Entering another man’s inclosure; injuring or annoying another; violating the divine law or moral duty.
tress: A knot or curl of hair; a ringlet. Fair tresses man’s imperial race ensnare.
tressure: In heraldry, a kind of border.
trestle: A movable form for supporting any thing.

In bridges, a frame consisting of two posts with a head or cross beam and braces, on which rest the string-pieces. [This is the use of the word in New England. It is vulgarly pronounced trussel or trussl.I . Trestle-trees, in a ship, are two strong bars of timber, fixed horizontally on the opposite sides of the lower mast-head, to support the frame of the top and the top-mast.
tret: In commerce, an allowance to purchasers, for waste or refuse matter, of four per cent on the weight of commodities. It is said this allowance is nearly discontinued.
trevet: A stool or other thing that is supported by three legs.
trey: A three at cards; a card of three spots.
tri: a prefix in words of Greek and Latin origin, signifies three.
triable: That may be tried; that may be subjected to trial or test.

That may undergo a judicial examination; that may properly come under the cognizance of a court. A cause may be triable before one court, which is not triable in another. In England, testamentary causes are triable in the ecclesiastical courts.
triacontahedral: Having thirty sides. In mineralogy, bounded by thirty rhombs.
triaconter: In ancient Greece, a vessel of thirty oars.
triad: The union of three; three united. In music, the common chord or harmony, consisting of the third, fifth and eighth.
trial: Any effort or exertion of strength for the purpose of ascertaining its effect, or what can be done. A man tries to lift a stone, and on trial finds he is not able. A team attempts to draw a load, and after unsuccessful trial, the attempt is relinquished.
Examination by a test; experiment; as in chimistry and metallurgy.
Experiment; act of examining by experience. In gardening and agriculture, we learn by trial what land will produce; and often, repeated trials are necessary.

Experience; suffering that puts strength, patience of faith to the test; afflictions or temptations that exercise and prove the graces or virtues of men. Others had trial of cruel mockings and scourgings. Hebrews 11:36.

In law, the examination of a cause in controversy between parties, before a proper tribunal. Trials are civil or criminal. Trial in civil causes, may be by record or inspection; it may be by witnesses and jury, or by the court. By the laws of England and of the United States, trial by jury, in criminal cases, is held sacred. No criminal can be legally deprived of that privilege.
triality: Three united; state of being three.
triangle: In geometry, a figure bounded by three lines, and containing three angles. The three angles of a triangle are equal to two right angles, or the number of degrees in a semicircle.

In geometry, a figure bounded by three lines, and containing three angles. The three angles of a triangle are equal to two right angles, or the number of degrees ina semicircle. If the three lines or sides of a triangle are all right, it is a plane or rectilinear triangle. If all the three sides are equal, it is an equilateral triangle. If two of the sides only are equal, it is an isosceles or equicrural triangle. If all the three sides are unequal, it is a scalene or scalenous triangle. If one of the angles is a right angle, the triangle is rectangular. If one of the angles is obtuse, the triangle is called obtusangular or amblygonous. If all the angles are acute, the triangle is acutangular or oxygonous. If the three lines of a triangle are all curves, the triangle is said to be curvilinear. If some of the sides are right and others curve, the triangle is said to be mixtilinear. If the
triangular: Having three angles. In botany, a triangular stem has three prominent longitudinal angles; a triangular leaf has three prominent angles, without any reference to their measurement or direction.
triangularly: After the form of a triangle.
triarian: Occupying the third post or place.
tribe: A family, race or series of generations, descending from the same progenitor and kept distinct, as in the case of the twelve tribes of Israel, descended from the twelve sons of Jacob.

A division, class or distinct portion of people, from whatever cause that distinction may have originated. The city of Athens was divided into ten tribes. Rome was originally divided into three tribes; afterward the people were distributed into thirty tribes, and afterwards into thirty five.

A number of things having certain characters or resemblances, in common; as a tribe of plants; a tribe of animals. Linneus distributed the vegetable kingdom into three tribesmonocotyledonous, dicotyledonous, and acotyledonous plants, and these he subdivided into gentes or nations. By recent naturalists, tribe has been used for a division of animals or vegetables, intermediate between order and genus. Cuvier divides his orders into families, and his families into tribes, including under the latter one or more genera. Leach, in his arrangement of insects, makes his tribes, on the contrary, the primary subdivisions of his orders, and his families subordinate to them, and immediately including the genera. Tribes of plants, in gardening, are such as are related to teach other by some natural affinity or resemblance; as by their duration, the annual, biennial, and perennial tribes; by their ro
A division; a number considered collectively.
A nation of savages; a body of rude people united under one leader or government; as the tribes of the six nations; the Seneca tribe in America.
triblet: A goldsmith’s tool for making rings.
triboulet: A goldsmith’s tool for making rings.
tribometer: An instrument to ascertain the degree of friction.
tribrach: In ancient prosody, a poetic foot of three short syllables, as melius.
tribracteate: Having three bracts about the flower.
tribulation: Severe affliction; distresses of life; vexations. In Scripture, it often denotes the troubles and distresses which proceed from persecution. When tribulation or persecution ariseth because of the word, he is offended. Matthew 13:21. In the world ye shall have tribulation. John 16:33.
tribunal: Properly, the seat of a judge; the bench on which a judge and his associates sit for administering justice.
More generally, a court of justice; as, the house of lords in England is the highest tribunal in the kingdom.
In France, a gallery or eminence in a church or other place, in which the musical performers are placed for a concert.
tribune: In ancient Rome, an officer or magistrate chosen by the people to protect them from the oppression of the patricians or nobles, and to defend their liberties against any attempts that might be made upon them by the senate and consuls. These magistrates were at first two, but their number was increased ultimately to ten. There were also military tribunes, officers of the army, each of whom commanded a division or legion. In the year of Rome 731, the senate transferred the authority of the tribunes to Augustus and his successors. There were also other officers called tribunes; as tribunes of the treasury, of the horse, of the making of arms.
In France, a pulpit or elevated place in the chamber of deputies, where a speaker stands to address the assembly.
tribunician: Pertaining to tribunes; as tribunician power or authority.
tribunitial: Pertaining to tribunes; as tribunician power or authority.
tributary: Paying tribute to another, either from compulsion, as an acknowledgment of submission, or to secure protection, or for the purpose of purchasing peace. The republic of Ragusa is tributary to the grand seignor. Many of the powers of Europe are tributary to the Barbary states.
Subject; subordinate. He, to grace his tributary gods-- .
Paid in tribute. No flatt’ry tunes these tributary lays.
Yielding supplies of any thing. The Ohio has many large tributary streams; and is itself tributary to the Mississippi.

One that pays tribute or a stated sum to a conquering power, for the purpose of securing peace and protection, or as an acknowledgment of submission, or for the purchase of security. What a reproach to nations that they should be the tributaries of Algiers!
tribute: An annual or stated sum of money or other valuable thing, paid by one prince or nation to another, either as an acknowledgment of submission, or as the price of peace and protection, or by virtue of some treaty. The Romans made all their conquered countries pay tribute, as do the Turks at this day; and in some countries the tribute is paid in children.
A personal contribution; as a tribute of respect.
tricapsular: In botany, three-capsuled; having three capsules to each flower.
trice: In seamen’s language, to haul and tie up by means of a small rope or line.
A very short time; an instant; a moment. If they get never so great spoil at any time, they waste the same in a trice. A man shall make his fortune in a trice.
trichotomous: Divided into three parts, or divided by threes; as a trichotomous stem.
trick: An artifice or stratagem for the purpose of deception; a fraudful contrivance for an evil purpose, or an underhand scheme to impose upon the world; a cheat or cheating. We hear of tricks in bargains, and tricks of state. He comes to me for counsel, and I show him a trick .
A dexterous artifice. On one nice trick depends the gen’ral fate.
Vicious practice; as the tricks of youth.
The sly artifice or legerdemain of a juggler; as the tricks of a merry Andrew.
A collection of cards laid together.
tricker: One who tricks; a deceiver; a cheat.
trickster: One who tricks; a deceiver; a cheat.
trickery: The art of dressing up; artifice; stratagem.
trickish: Artful in making bargains; given to deception and cheating; knavish.
trickle: To flow in a small gentle stream; to run down; as, tears trickle down the cheek; water trickles from the eaves. Fast beside there trickled softly down . A gentle stream.
trickling: Flowing down in a small gentle stream.
The act of flowing in a small gentle stream. He wakened by the trickling of his blood.
tricliniary: Pertaining to a couch for dining, or to the ancient mode of reclining at table.
tricoccous: A tricoccous or three-grained capsule is one which is swelling out in three protuberances, internally divided into three cells, with one seed in each; as in Euphorbia.
tricuspidate: In botany, three-pointed; ending in three points; as a tricuspidate stamen.
tride: Among hunters, short and ready; fleet; as a tride pace.
trident: In mythology, a kind of scepter or spear with three prongs, which the fables of antiquity put into the hands of Neptune, the deity of the ocean.
tridiapason: In music, a triple octave or twenty second.
tridodecahedral: In crystallography, presenting three ranges of faces, one above another, each containing twelve faces.
triduan: Lasting three days, or happening every third day.
triennial: Continuing three years; as triennial parliaments.

Happening every three years; as triennial elections. Triennial elections and parliaments were established in England in 1695; but these were discontinued in 1717, and septennial elections and parliaments were adopted, which still continue.
trier: One who tries; one who makes experiments; one who examines any thing by a test or standard.
One who tries judicially; a judge who tries a person or cause; a juryman.
A test; that which tries or approves.
In law, a person appointed by the court to examine whether a challenge to a panel of jurors, or to any juror, is just. The triors are two indifferent persons.
trierarch: In ancient Greece, the commander of a trireme; also, a commissioner who was obliged to build ships and furnish them at his own expense.
trieterical: Triennial; kept or occurring once in three years.
trifallow: To plow land the third time before sowing.
trifid: In botany, divided into three parts by linear sinuses with strait margins; three-cleft.
trifle: A thing of very little value or importance; a word applicable to any thing and every thing of this character. With such poor trifles playing. Moments make the year, and trifles. Trifles . Are to the jealous confirmations strong.
To act or talk without seriousness, gravity, weight or dignity; to act or talk with levity. They trifle, and they beat the air about nothing which toucheth us.

To indulge in light amusements. To trifle with, to mock; to play the fool with; to treat without respect a ae aa or seriousness. To trifle with, to spend in vanity; to waste. To trifle away, to no good purpose; as, to trifle with time, or to trifle away time; to trifle with advantages.
trifler: One who trifles or acts with levity.
trifling: Acting or talking with levity, or without seriousness or being in earnest.
a. Being of small value or importance; trivial; as a trifling debt; a trifling affair.
Employment about things of no importance.
triflingly: In a trifling manner; with levity; without seriousness or dignity.
triflorous: Three-flowered; bearing three flowers; as a triflorous peduncle.
triform: 2. Having a triple form or shape; as the triform countenance of the moon.
trigamy: State of being married tree times; or the state of having three husbands or three wives at the same time.
trigger: A catch to hold the wheel of a carriage on a declivity.
The catch of a musket or pistol; the part which being pulled, looses the lock for striking fire.
trigintals: Trentals; the number of thirty masses to be said for the dead.
triglyph: An ornament in the frieze of the Doric column, repeated at equal intervals. Each triglyph consists of two entire gutters or channels, cut to a right angle, called glyphs, and separated by three interstices, called femora.
trigon: A triangle; a term used in astrology; also, trine, an aspect of two planets distant 120 degrees from each other.
Akind of triangular lyre or harp.
trigonal: Triangular; having three angles or corners.
In botany, having three prominent longitudinal angles.
trigonous: Triangular; having three angles or corners.
In botany, having three prominent longitudinal angles.
trigonometrical: Pertaining to trigonometry; performed by or according to the rules of trigonometry.
trigonometrically: According to the rules or principles of trigonometry.
trigonometry: The measuring of triangles; the science of determining the sides and angles of triangles, by means of certain parts which are given. When this science is applied to the solution of plane triangles, it is called plane trigonometry; when its application is to spherical triangles, it is called spherical trigonometry.
trigyn: In botany, a plant having three pistils.
trihedron: A figure having three equal sides.
trijugous: In botany, having three pairs. A trijiugous leaf is a pinnate leaf with three pairs of leaflets.
triliteral: Consisting of three letters; as a triliteral root or word.
A word consisting of three letters.
trill: A quaver; a shake of the voice in singing, or of the sound of an instrument.
To utter with a quavering or tremulousness of voice; to shake. The sober-suited songstress trills her lay.
To flow in a small stream, or in drops rapidly succeeding each other; to trickle. And now and then an ample tear trill’d down . Her delicate cheek.
To shake or quaver; to play in tremulous vibrations of sound. To judge of trilling notes and tripping feet.
trilling: Uttering with a quavering or shake.
trillion: tril’yun. The product of a million multiplied by a million, and that product multiplied by a million; or the product of the square of a million multiplied by a million. Thus 1,000,000 x 1,000,000=1,000,000,000,000, and this product multiplied by a million= 1,000,000,000,000,000,000.
trilocular: In botany, three-celled; having three cells for seeds; as a trilocular capsule.
trim: Firm; compact; tight; snug; being in good order. We say of a ship, she is trim, or trim-built; every thing about the man is trim. We say of a person, he is trim, when his body is well shaped and firm; and we say, his dress is trim, when it sits closely to his body and appears tight and snug; and of posture we say, a man or a soldier is trim, when he stands erect. It is particularly applicable to soldiers, and in Saxon, truma is a troop or body of soldiers.

Firm; compact; tight; snug; being in good order. We say of a ship, she is trim, or trim-built; every thing about the man is trim. We say of a person, he is trim, when his body is well shaped and firm; and we say, his dress is trim, when it sits closely to his body and appears tight and snug; and of posture we Say, a man or a soldier is trim, when he stands erect. It is particularly applicable to soldiers, and in Saxon, truma is a troop or body of soldiers.
In a general sense, to make right, that is, to put in due order for any purpose. The hermit trimm’d his little fire.
To dress; to put the body in a proper state. I was trimm’d in Julia’s gown.
To decorate; to invest or embellish with extra ornaments; as, to trim a gown with lace.
trimeter: A poetical division of verse, consisting of three measures.
Consisting of three poetical measures, forming an iambic of six feet.
trimetrical: Consisting of three poetical measures, forming an iambic of six feet.
trimmed: Put in good order; dressed; ornamented; clipped; shaved; balanced; rebuked.
trimmer: A piece of timber fitted in. All the joists and the trimmers for the staircase-- .&
trimming: Putting in due order; dressing; decorating; pruning; balancing; fluctuating between parties.
Ornamental appendages to a garment, as lace, ribbons and the like.
trimness: Neatness; snugness; the state of being close and in good order.
trine: Threefold; as trine dimension, that is, length, breadth and thickness.
In astrology, the aspect of planets distant from each other 120 degrees, forming the figure of a trigon or triangle.
To put in the aspect of a trine.
trinervate: In botany, having three nerves or unbranched vessels meeting behind or beyond the base.
trinerve: In botany, a trinerved or three-nerved leaf, has three nerves or unbranched vessels meeting in the base of the leaf.
trinerved: In botany, a trinerved or three-nerved leaf, has three nerves or unbranched vessels meeting in the base of the leaf.
tringle: In architecture, a little square member or ornament, as a listel, reglet, platband and the like, but particularly a little member fixed exactly over every triglyph.
trinitarian: Pertaining to the Trinity, or to the doctrine of the Trinity.
One who believes the doctrine of the Trinity.
One of an order of religious, who made it their business to redeem christians from infidels.
trinity: In theology, the union of three persons in one Godhead, the Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit. In my whole essay, there is not any thing like an objection against the Trinity: .
trinket: A small ornament, as a jewel, a ring and the like.
A thing of little value; tackle; tools.
trinomial: In mathematics, a trinomial root, is a root consisting of three parts, connected by the signs + or -. Thus x+y+z, or atb-c.
A root of three terms or parts.
trio: A concert of three parts; three united.
triobolar: Of the value of three oboli; mean; worthless.
trioctahedral: 2. In crystallography, presenting three ranges of faces, one above another, each range containing eight faces.
trioctile: In astrology, an aspect of two planets with regard to the earth, when they are three octants or eight parts of a circle, that is, degrees, distant from each other.
trior: In law, a person appointed by the court to examine whether a challenge to a panel of jurors, or to any juror, is just. The triors are two indifferent persons.
trip: To supplant; to cause to fall by striking the feet suddenly from under the person; usually followed by up; as, to trip up a man in wrestling; to trip up the heels.
To supplant; to overthrow by depriving of support.
To loose an anchor from the bottom by its cable or buoy- rope.
To stumble; to strike the foot against something, so as to lose the step and come near to fall; or to stumble and fall.
tripartite: Divided into three parts. In botany, a tripartite leaf is one which is divided into three parts down to the base, but not wholly separate.
Having three corresponding parts or copies; as indentures tripartite.
tripartition: A division by three, or the taking of a third part of any number or quantity.
tripe: Properly, the entrails; but in common usage, the large stomach of ruminating animals, prepared for food.
tripennate: In botany, a tripinnate leaf is a species of superdecompound leaf, when a petiole has bipinnate leaves ranged on each side of it, as in common fern.
tripinnate: In botany, a tripinnate leaf is a species of superdecompound leaf, when a petiole has bipinnate leaves ranged on each side of it, as in common fern.
tripetalous: In botany, three-petaled; having three petals or flower leaves.
triphthong: A coalition of three vowels in one compound sound, or in one syllable, as in adieu
triphthongal: Pertaining to a triphthong; consisting of a triphthong.
triphyllous: In botany, three-leaved; having three leaves.
triple: Threefold; consisting of three united; as a triple knot; a triple tie. By thy triple shape as thou are seen-- .
Treble; three times repeated. Triple time, in music, is that in which each bar is divided into three measures or equal parts, as three minims, three crotchets, three quavers.
To treble; to make threefold or thrice as much or as many.
triplet: Three of a kind, or three united.
In poetry, three verses rhyming together.
In music, three notes sung or played in the time of two.
triplicate: Made thrice as much; threefold. Triplicate ratio, is the ratio which cubes bear to each other.
triplication: The act of trebling or making threefold, or adding three together.
In the civil law, the same as sur-rejoinder in common law.
triplicity: Trebleness; the state of being threefold.
triply-ribbed: In botany, having a pair of large ribs branching off from the main one above the base, as in the leaves of many species of sunflower.
tripod: A bench, stool or seat supported by three legs, on which the priest and sibyls in ancient times were placed to render oracles.
tripoli: In mineralogy, a mineral originally brought from Tripoli, used in polishing stones and metals. It has a dull argillaceous appearance, but is not compact. It has a fine hard grain, but does not soften by water, or mix with it. It is principally composed of silex.
tripper: One who trips or supplants; one that walks nimbly.
tripping: The loosing of an anchor from the ground by its cable or buoy- rope.
trippingly: Nimbly; with a light nimble quick step; with agility. Sing and dance it trippingly. Speak the speech trippingly on the tongue.
triptote: In grammar, a name having three cases only.
tripudiary: Pertaining to dancing; performed by dancing.
tripyramid: In mineralogy, a genus of spars, the body of which is composed of single pyramids, each of three sides, affixed by their base to some solid body.
trireme: A galley or vessel with three benches or ranks of oars on a side.
trirhomboidal: Having the form of three rhombs.
trisacramentarian: One of a religious sect who admit of three sacraments and no more.
trisagion: A hymn in which the word holy is repeated three times.
trisect: To cut or divide into three equal parts.
trisection: The division of a thing into three parts; particularly in geometry, the division of an angle into three equal parts.
trisepalous: In botany, having three sepals to a calyx.
trispast: In mechanics, a machine with three pulleys for raising great weights.
trispaston: In mechanics, a machine with three pulleys for raising great weights.
trispermous: Three-seeded; containing three seeds; as a trispermous capsule.
tripersonality: The state of existing in three persons in one Godhead.
trisyllabic: Pertaining to a trisyllable; consisting of three syllables; as a trisyllabic word or root.
trisyllabical: Pertaining to a trisyllable; consisting of three syllables; as a trisyllabic word or root.
trisyllable: A word consisting of three syllables.
trite: Worn out; common; used till so common as to have lost its novelty and interest; as a trite remark; a trite subject.
triteness: Commonness; staleness; a state of being worn out; as the triteness of an observation or a subject.
triternate: 2. Having three biternate leaves, or the divisions of a triple petiole subdivided into threes; a species of superdecompound leaf.
tritheism: The opinion or doctrine that there are three Gods in the Godhead.
tritheist: One who believes that there are three distinct Gods in the Godhead, that is, three distinct substances, essences of hypostases.
trithing: One of the divisions of the county of York in England, which is divided into three parts. It is now called Riding.
triton: In mythology, a fabled sea demi-god, supposed to be the trumpeter of Neptune. He is represented by poets and painters as half man and half fish.
A genus of the molluscal order of worms.
A bird of the West Indies, famous for its notes.
tritone: In music, a false concord, consisting of three tones, two major and one minor tone, or of two tones and two semitones; a dissonant interval.
tritoxyd: In chimistry, a substance oxydized in the third degree.
triturable: Capable of being reduced to a fine powder by pounding, rubbing or grinding.
triturate: To rub or grind to a very fine powder, and properly to a finer powder than that made by pulverization.
triturated: Reduced to a very fine powder.
triturating: Grinding or reducing to a very fine powder.
trituration: The act of reducing to a fine powder by grinding.
triturium: A vessel for separating liquors of different densities.
triumph: Among the ancient Romans, a pompous ceremony performed in honor of a victorious general, who was allowed to enter the city crowned, originally with laurel, but in later times with gold, bearing a truncheon in one hand and a branch of laurel in the other, riding in a chariot drawn by two white horses, and followed by the kings, princes and generals whom he had vanquished, loaded with chains and insulted by mimics and buffoons. The triumph was of two kinds, the greater and the less. The lesser triumph was granted for a victory over enemies of less considerable power, and was called an ovation.
State of being victorious. Hercules from Spain . Arriv’d in triumph, from Geryon slain.
Victory; conquest. The vain coquets the trifling triumphs boast.
Joy or exultation for success. Great triumph and rejoicing was in heav’n.
A card that takes all others; now written trump, which see.
triumphal: Pertaining to triumph; used in a triumph; as a triumphal crown or car; a triumphal arch.
triumphant: Celebrating victory; as a triumphant chariot.
Rejoicing as for victory. Successful beyond hope to lead you forth . Triumphant out of this infernal pit.
Victorious; graced with conquest. So shall it be in the church triumphant. Athena, war’s triumphant maid-- .
Celebrating victory; expressing joy for success; as a triumphant song.
triumphantly: In a triumphant manner; with the joy and exultation that proceeds from victory or success. Through armed ranks’ triumphantly she drives.
Victoriously; with success. Triumphantly tread on thy country’s ruin.
triumpher: One who triumphs or rejoices for victory; one who vanquishes.
One who was honored with a triumph in Rome.
triumphing: Celebrating victory with pomp; vanquishing; rejoicing for victory; insulting on an advantage.
triumvir: One of three men united in office. The triumvirs, L. triumviri, of Rome, were three men who jointly obtained the sovereign power in Rome. The first of these were Caesar, Crassus and Pompey.
triumvirate: A coalition of three men; particularly, the union of three men who obtained the government of the Roman empire.
Government by three men in coalition.
triune: Three in one; an epithet applied to God, to express the unity of the Godhead in a trinity of persons.
triverbial: Triverbial days, in the Roman calendar, were juridical or court days, days allowed to the pretor for hearing causes; called also dies fasti. There were only twenty eight in the year.
trivial: Trifling; of little worth or importance; inconsiderable; as a trivial subject; a trivial affair.

Worthless; vulgar. Trivial name, in natural history, the common name for the species, which added to the generic name forms the complete denomination of the species; the specific name. Thus in Lathyrus aphaca, Lathyrus is the generic name, and aphaca the trivial or specific name, and the two combined form the complete denomination of the species. Linne at first applied the term specific name to the essential character of the species, now called the specific definition or difference; but it is now applied solely to the trivial name.
trivially: Lightly; inconsiderably; in a trifling degree.
troat: To cry, as a buck in rutting time.
The cry of a buck in rutting time.
trocar: A surgical instrument for tapping dropsical persons and the like.
trochaic: In poetry, consisting of trochees; as trochaic measure or verse.
trochaical: In poetry, consisting of trochees; as trochaic measure or verse.
trochanter: In anatomy, the trochanters are two processes of the thigh bone, called major and minor, the major on the outside, and the minor on the inside.
troche: A form of medicine in a cake or tablet, or a stiff paste cut into proper portions and dried. It is made by mixing the medicine with sugar and the mucilage of gum tragacanth, intended to be gradually dissolved in the mouth and slowly swallowed, as a demulcent to sheath the epiglottis, and as a remedy for the bronchocele.
trochee: In verse, a foot of two syllables, the first long and the second short.
trochilic: Having power to draw out or turn round.
trochilus: An aquatic bird, a swift runner, with long legs, which is said to get its meat out of the crocodile’s mouth.
Aname given to the golden crowned wren.<
In zoology, the humming bird or honeysucker, a kind of beautiful little birds, natives of America.
In architecture, a hollow ring round a column; called also scotia, and by workmen, the casement.
trochil: An aquatic bird, a swift runner, with long legs, which is said to get its meat out of the crocodile’s mouth.
Aname given to the golden crowned wren.<
In zoology, the humming bird or honeysucker, a kind of beautiful little birds, natives of America.
In architecture, a hollow ring round a column; called also scotia, and by workmen, the casement.
trochings: The small branches on the top of a deer’s head.
trochisch: A kind of tablet or lozenge.
trochite: In natural history, a kind of figured fossil stone, resembling parts of plants, called St. Cuthbert’s beads. These stones are usually of a brownish color; they break like spar, and are easily dissolved in vinegar. Their figure is generally cylindrical, sometimes a little tapering. Two, three or more of these joined, constitute an entrochus.
Fossil remains of the shells called trochus.
trochlea: A pulley-like cartilage, through which the tendon of the trochleary muscle passes.
trochleary: Pertaining to the trochlea; as the trochleary muscle, the superior oblique muscle of the eye; the trochleary nerve, the pathetic nerve, which goes to that muscle.
trochoid: In geometry, a curve generated by the motion of a wheel; the cycloid.
trod: of tread. Jerusalem shall be trodden down by the Gentiles. Luke 21:24.
trodden: of tread. Jerusalem shall be trodden down by the Gentiles. Luke 21:24.
troglodyte: The Troglodytes were a people of Ethiopia, represented by the ancients as living in caves, about whom we have many fables.
troll: To move in a circular direction; to roll; to move volubly; to turn; to drive about. They learn to roll the eye, and troll the tongue. Troll about the bridal bow.
To roll; to run about; as, to troll in a coach and six.
Among anglers, to fish for pikes with a rod whose line runs on a wheel or pulley.
trolling: Rolling; turning; driving about; fishing with a rod and reel.
trollop: A stroller; a loiterer; a woman loosely dressed; a slattern.
trollopee: Formerly, a loose dress for females.
tromp: A blowing machine formed of a hollow tree, used in furnaces.
tronage: Formerly, a toll or duty paid for weighing wool.
tronator: An officer in London, whose business was to weigh wool.
tronco: A term in Italian music, directing a note or sound to be cut short, or just uttered and then discontinued.
trone: A provincial word in some parts of England for a small drain.
troop: A collection of people; a company; a number; a multitude. Genesis 49:19; 2 Samuel 23:11; Hosea 7:1. That which should accompany old age, As honor, love, obedience, troops of friends, I must not look to have.

A body of soldiers. But applied to infantry, it is now used in the plural, troops, and this word signifies soldiers in general, whether more or less numerous, including infantry, cavalry and artillery. We apply the word to a company, a regiment or an army. The captain ordered his troops to halt; the colonel commanded his troops to wheel and take a position on the flank; the general ordered his troops to attack; the troops of France amounted to 400.
Troop, in the singular, a small body or company of cavalry, light horse or dragoons, commanded by a captain.
To collect in numbers. Armies at the call of trumpet, Troop to their standard.
trooper: A private or soldier in a body of cavalry; a horse soldier.
trooping: Moving together in a crowd; marching in a body.
trope: In rhetoric, a word or expression used in a different sense from that which it properly signifies; or a word changed from its original signification to another, for the sake of giving life or emphasis to an idea, as when we call a stupid fellow an ass, or a shrewd man a fox. Tropes are chiefly of four kinds, metaphor, metonymy, synecdoche, and irony. Some authors make figure the genus, of which trope is a species; others make them different things, defining trope to be a change of sense, and figure to be any ornament, except what becomes so by such change.
trophied: Adorned with trophies. --The trophied arches, storied halls invade.
trophy: Among the ancients, a pile of arms taken from a vanquished enemy, raised on the field of battle by the conquerors; also, the representation of such a pile in marble, on medals and the like; or according to others, trophies were trees planted in conspicuous places of the conquered provinces, and hung with the spoils of the enemy, in memory of the victory. Hence,

Any thing taken and preserved as a memorial of victory, as arms, flags, standards and the like, taken from an enemy. Around the posts hung helmets, darts and spears, And captive chariots, axes, shields and bars, And broken beaks of ships, the trophies of their wars.
In architecture, an ornament representing the stem of a tree, charged or encompassed with arms and military weapons, offensive and defensive.
Something that is evidence of victory; memorial of conquest. Present every hearer to Christ as a trophy of grace.
trophy-money: A duty paid in England annually by house-keepers, towards providing harness, drums, colorsfor the militia.
tropic: In astronomy, a circle of the sphere drawn through a solstitial point, parallel to the equator; or the line which bounds the sun’s declination from the equator, north or south. This declination is twenty-three degrees and a half nearly. There are two tropics; the tropic of Cancer, on the north of the equator, and the tropic of Capricorn on the south.
Tropics, in geography, are two lesser circles of the globe, drawn parallel to the equator through the beginning of Cancer and of Capricorn.
tropical: Pertaining to the tropics; being within the tropics; as tropical climates; tropical latitudes; tropical heat; tropical winds.
Incident to the tropics; as tropical diseases.

Figurative; rhetorically changed from its proper or original sense. The foundation of all parables is some analogy or similitude between the tropical or allusive part of the parable, and the thing intended by it. Tropical writing or hieroglyphic, is such as represents a thing by qualities which resemble it.
tropically: In a tropical or figurative manner.
tropic-bird: An aquatic fowl of the genus Phaeton, with a long slender tail and remarkable powers of flight.
tropist: One who explains the Scriptures by tropes and figures of speech; one who deals in tropes.
tropological: Varied by tropes; changed from the original import of the words.
tropology: A rhetorical mode of speech, including tropes, or change from the original import of the word.
trot: To move faster than in walking, as a horse or other quadruped, by lifting one fore foot and the hind foot of the opposite side at the same time.
To walk or move fast; or to run. He that rises late must trot all day, and will scarcely overtake his business at night.

The pace of a horse or other quadruped, when he lifts one fore foot and the hind foot of the opposite side at the same time. This pace is the same as that of a walk, but more rapid. The trot is often a jolting hard motion, but in some horses, it is as easy as the amble or pace, and has a more stately appearance.
troth: Belief; faith; fidelity; as, to plight one’s troth.
Truth; verity; veracity; as in troth; by my troth.
troth-plight: The act of betrothing or plighting faith.
trotter: A beast that trots, or that usually trots.
trotting: Moving with a trot; walking fast, or running.
trouble: To agitate; to disturb; to put into confused motion. God looking forth will trouble all his host. An angel went down at a certain season into the pool, and troubled the water. John 5:4.
To disturb; to perplex. Never trouble yourself about those faults which age will cure.
To afflict; to grieve; to distress. Those that trouble me, rejoice when I am moved.Psalm 13:4.
To busy; to cause to be much engaged or anxious. Martha, thou art careful, and troubled about many things. Luke 10:41.
To tease; to vex; to molest. The boy so troubles me, ‘Tis past enduring.
troubled: trub’ld. Disturbed; agitated; afflicted; annoyed; molested.
troubler: trub’ler. One who disturbs; one who afflicts or molests; a disturber; as a troubler of the peace. The rich troublers of the world’s repose.
troublesome: trub’lsome. Giving trouble or disturbance; molesting; annoying; vexatious. In warm climates, insects are very troublesome.
Burdensome; tiresome; wearisome. My mother will never be troublesome to me.
Giving inconvenience to. I wish not to be troublesome as a guest.
Teasing; importunate; as a troublesome applicant.
troublesomely: trub’lsomely. In a manner or degree to give trouble; vexatiously.
troublesomeness: Vexatiousness; the quality of giving trouble or of molesting.
troubling: trub’ling. Disturbing; agitating; molesting; annoying; afflicting.
trub’ling. The act of disturbing or putting in commotion. John 5:4.
troublous: trub’lus. Agitated; tumultuous; full of commotion. A tall ship toss’d in troublous seas.
Full of trouble or disorder; tumultuous; full of affliction. The street shall be built again, and the wall, even in troublous times. Daniel 9:25.
trough: A vessel hollow longitudinally, or a large log or piece of timber excavated longitudinally on the upper side; used for various purposes.
A canoe; the rude boat of uncivilized men.
The channel that conveys water, as in mills. The trough of the sea, the hollow between waves.
trounce: trouns. To punish, or to beat severely.
trouse: A kind of trowsers worn by children.
trout: A river fish of the genus Salmo, variegated with spots, and esteemed as most delicate food.
trout-colored: White with spots of black, bay or sorrel; as a trout-colored horse.
trout-stream: A stream in which trout bread.
trover: Trover is properly the finding of any thing. Hence,
In law, the gaining possession of any goods, whether by finding or by other means.

An action which a man has against another who has found or obtained possession of any of his goods, and who refuses to deliver them on demand. This is called an action of trover and conversion. In this case, the trover or finding is an immaterial fact, but the plaintiff must prove his own property, and the possession and conversion of the goods by the defendant.
trow: To believe; to trust; to think or suppose.
is used in the imperative, as a word of inquiry. What means the fool, trow?
trowel: A mason’s tool, used in spreading and dressing mortar, and breaking bricks to shape them.
A gardener’s tool, somewhat like a trowel, made of iron and scooped; used in taking up plants and for other purposes.
trowsers: s as z. A loose garment worn by males, extending from the waist to the knee or to the ankle, and covering the lower limbs.
troy: The weight by which gold and silver, jewelsare weighed. In this weight, 20 grains = a scruple, 3 scruples = a dram, 8 drams = an ounce, and ounces = one pound.
troy-weight: The weight by which gold and silver, jewelsare weighed. In this weight, 20 grains = a scruple, 3 scruples = a dram, 8 drams = an ounce, and ounces = one pound.
truant: Idle; wandering from business; loitering; as a truant boy. While truant Jove, in infant price, Play’d barefoot on Olympus’side.
To idle away time; to loiter or be absent from employment.
truce: In war, a suspension of arms by agreement of the commanders; a temporary cessation of hostilities, either for negotiation or other purpose.
Intermission of action, pain or contest; temporary cessation; short quiet. There he may find . Truce to his restless thoughts.
truce-breaker: One who violates a truce, covenant or engagement. 2 Timothy 3:3.
truck: To exchange commodities; to barter. Our traders truck with the Indians, giving them whiskey and trinkets for skins.
To exchange; to give in exchange; to barter; as, to truck knives for gold dust.
A small wooden wheel not bound with iron; a cylinder.
A small wheel; hence trucks, a low carriage for carrying goods, stoneIndeed this kind of carriage is often called a truck, in the singular.
trucker: One who traffics by exchange of goods.
truckle: To yield or bend obsequiously to the will of another; to submit; to creep. Small states must truckle to large ones. Religion itself is forced to truckle with worldly policy.
truckle-bed: A bed that runs on wheels and may be pushed under another; a trundle-bed.
truckling: Yielding obsequiously to the will of another.
truculent: Fierce; savage; barbarous; as the truculent inhabitants of Scythia.
Cruel; destructive; as a truculent plague.
trudge: To travel on foot. The father rode; the son trudged on behind.
To travel or march with labor. --And trudg’d to Rome upon my naked feet.
true: Conformable to fact; being in accordance with the actual state of things; as a true relation or narration; a true history. A declaration is true, when it states the facts. In this sense, true is opposed to false.

Genuine; pure; real; not counterfeit, adulterated or false; as true balsam; the true bark; true love of country; a true christian. --The true light which lighteth every man that cometh into the world. John 1:9.

Faithful; steady in adhering to friends, to promises, to a prince or to the state; loyal; not false, fickle or perfidious; as a true friend; a true lover; a man true to his king, true to his country, true to his word; a husband true to his wife; a wife true to her husband; a servant true to his master; an officer true to his charge.
Free from falsehood; as a true witness.
Honest; not fraudulent; as good men and true. If king Edward be as true and just-- .
trueborn: Of genuine birth; having a right by birth to any title; as a true born Englishman.
truebred: Of a genuine or right breed; as a truebred beast.
Being of genuine breeding or education; as a truebred gentleman.
truehearted: Being of a faithful heart; honest; sincere; not faithless or deceitful; as a truehearted friend.
truelove-knot: A knot composed of lines united with many involutions; the emblem of interwoven affection or engagements.
trueness: Exactness; as the trueness of a line.
truepenny: A familiar phrase for an honest fellow.
truffle: A subterraneous vegetable production, or a kind of mushroom, of a fleshy fungous structure and roundish figure; an esculent substance, much esteemed. It is of the genus Tuber.
truffle-worm: A worm found in truffles, the larva of a fly.
trug: A hod. This is our trough and tray; the original pronunciation being retained in some parts of England. The word was also used formerly for a measure of wheat, as much, I suppose as was carried in a trough; three trugs making two bushels.
truism: An undoubted or self-evident truth. Trifling truisms clothed in great swelling words of vanity-- .
trullization: The laying of strata of plaster with a trowel.
truly: In fact; in deed; in reality.
According to truth; in agreement with fact; as, to see things truly; the facts are truly represented.
Sincerely; honestly; really; faithfully; as, to be truly attached to a lover. The citizens are truly loyal to their prince or their country.
Exactly; justly; as, to estimate truly the weight of evidence.
trump: A trumpet; a wind instrument of music; a poetical word used for trumpet. It is seldom used in prose, in common discourse; but is used in Scripture, where it seems peculiarly appropriate to the grandeur of the subject. At the last trump; for the trumpet shall sound, and the dead shall be raised. 1 Corinthians 15:52; 1 Thessalonians 4:16.
A winning card; one of the suit of cards which takes any of the other suits.
An old game with cares. To put to the trumps, To put on the trumps, to reduce to the last expedient, or to the utmost exertion of power.
To take with a trump card.
To obtrude; also, to deceive. To trump up, to devise; to seek and collect from every quarter.
trumpery: Useless matter; things worn out and cast side.
trumpet: A wind instrument of music, used chiefly in war and military exercises. It is very useful also at sea, in speaking with ships. There is a speaking trumpet, and a hearing trumpet. They both consist of long tubular bodies, nearly in the form of a parabolic conoid, with wide mouths. The trumpet’s loud clangor . Excites us to arms.
In the military style, a trumpeter. He wisely desired that a trumpet might be first sent for a pass.
One who praises or propagates praise, or is the instrument or propagating it. A great politician was pleased to be the trumpet of his praises.
To publish by sound of trumpet; also, to proclaim; as, to trumpet good tidings. They did nothing but publish and trumpet all the reproaches they could devise against the Irish.
trumpeter: One who proclaims, publishes or denounces. These men are good trumpeters.

A bird, a variety of the domestic pigeon. Also, a bird of South America, the agami, of the genus Psophia, about the size of the domestic fowl; so called from its uttering a hollow noise, like that of a trumpet.
trumpet-fish: A fish of the genus Centriscus, (C. scolopax;) called also the bellows fish.
trumpet-flower: A flower of the genus Bignonia, and another of the genus Lonicera.
trumpet honeysuckle: A plant of the genus Lonicera.
trumpet-shell: The name of a genus of univalvular shells, of the form of a trumpet, (Buccinum, Linne.)
trumpet-tongued: Having a tongue vociferous as a trumpet.
truncate: In botany, appearing as if cut off at the tip; ending in a transverse line; as a truncate leaf.
To cut off; to lop; to maim.
truncated: Cut off; cut short; maimed. A truncated cone is one whose vertex is cut off by a plane parallel to its base.
Appearing as if cut off; plane; having no edge; as a mineral substance.
truncation: The act of lopping or cutting off.
truncheon: A short staff; a club; a cudgel; a battoon; used by kings and great officers as a mark of command. The marshal’s truncheon nor the judge’s’ robe.
To beat with a truncheon; to cudgel.
truncheoneer: A person armed with a truncheon.
trundle: To roll, as a thing on little wheels; as, to trundle a bed or a gun-carriage.
To roll, as on little wheels; as, a bed trundles under another.
A round body; a little wheel, or a kind or low cart with small wooden wheels.
trundle-bed: A bed that is moved on trundles or little wheels; called also truckle- bed.
trundle-tail: A round tail; a dog so called from his tail.
trunk: The stem or body of a tree, severed form its roots. This is the proper sense of the word. But surprising as it may seem, it is used most improperly to signify the stem of a standing tree or vegetable, in general.
The body of an animal without the limbs.
The main body of any thing; as the trunk of a vein or of an artery, as distinct from the branches.
The snout or proboscis of an elephant; the limb or instrument with which he feeds himself.
A slender, oblong, hollow body, joined to the fore part of the head of many insects by means of which they suck the blood of animals or the juices of vegetables.
trumk: To lop off; to curtail; to truncate.
trunnion: The trunnions of a piece of ordnance, are two knobs which project from the opposite sides of a piece, whether gun, mortar or howitzer, and serve to support it on the cheeks of the carriage.
trunnion-plate: The trunnion plates are two plates in traveling carriages, mortars and howitzers, which cover the upper parts of the side-pieces, and go under the trunnions.
trunnion-ring: A ring on a cannon next before the trunnions.
trusion: tru’zhon. The act of pushing or thrusting.
truss: In a general sense, a bundle; as a truss of hay or straw. A truss of hay in England is half a hundred. A truss of straw is of different weights in different places.
In surgery, a bandage or apparatus used in cases of ruptures, to keep up the reduced parts and hinder further protrusion, and for other purposes.
Among botanists, a truss or bunch is a tuft of flowers formed at the top of the main stalk or stem of certain plants.
In navigation, a machine to pull a lower yard close to its mast and retain it firmly in that position.
trust: Confidence; a reliance or resting of the mind on the integrity, veracity, justice, friendship or other sound principle of another person. He that putteth his trust in the Lord shall be safe. Proverbs 29:25.
He or that which is the ground of confidence. O Lord God, thou art my trust from my youth. Psalm 77:5.
Charge received in confidence. Reward them well, if they observe their trust.
That which is committed to one’s care. Never violate a sacred trust.
Confident opinion of any event. His trust was with th’ Eternal to be deem’d . Equal in strength.
trusted: Confided in; relied on; depended on; applied to persons.
Sold on credit; as goods or property.
Delivered in confidence to the care of another; as letters or goods trusted to a carrier or bailee.
trustee: A person to whom any thing or business is committed, in confidence that he will discharge his duty. The trustee of an estate is one to whom it is devised or granted in trust, or for the use of another.
A person to whom is confided the management of an institution; as the trustees of a college or of an academy. N .
truster: One who trusts or gives credit.
trustiness: That quality of a person by which he deserves the confidence of others; fidelity; faithfulness; honesty; as the trustiness of aservant.
trusting: Confiding in; giving credit; relying on.
trusty: That may be safely trusted; that justly deserves confidence; fit to be confided in; as a trusty servant.
That will not fail; strong; firm; as a trusty sword.
truth: Conformity to fact or reality; exact accordance with that which is, or has been, or shall be. The truth of history constitutes its whole value. We rely on the truth of the scriptural prophecies. My mouth shall speak truth. Proverbs 8:7. Sanctify them through thy truth; thy word is truth. John 17:17.
True state of facts or things. The duty of a court of justice is to discover the truth. Witnesses are sworn to declare the truth, the whole truth, and nothing but the truth.
Conformity of words to thoughts, which is called moral truth. Shall truth fail to keep her word?
Veracity; purity from falsehood; practice of speaking truth; habitual disposition to speak truth; as when we say, a man is a man of truth.
truttaceous: Pertaining to the trout; as fish of the truttaceous genus.
try: To exert strength; to endeavor; to make an effort; to attempt. Try to learn; try to lift a weight. The horses tried to draw the load.
To examine; to make experiment on; to prove by experiment. Come, try upon yourselves what you have seen me.
To experience; to have knowledge by experience of. Or try the Libyan heat, or Scythian cold.
To prove by a test; as, to try weights and measures by a standard; to try one’s opinions by the divine oracles.
To act upon as a test. The fire sev’n times tried this.
trying: Examining by searching or comparison with a test; proving; using; straining.
a. Adapted to try, or put to severe trial.
try-sail: A sail used by a ship in a storm; literally the strain-sail.
tub: An open wooden vessel formed with staves, heading and hoops; used for various domestic purposes, as for washing, for making cheese.
A state of salivation; so called because the patient was formerly sweated in a tub.
A certain quantity; as a tub of tea, which is 60 pounds; a tub of camphor, from 56 to 80 pounds; a tub of vermilion, from 3 to hundred pounds.
A wooden vessel in which vegetables are planted, for the sake of being movable and set in a house in cold weather.
To plant or set in a tub.
tubber: In Cornwall, a mining instrument, called in other places a beele. The man who uses this tool is called tubber-man or beel-man.
tube: A pipe; a siphon; a canal or conduit; a hollow cylinder, either of wood, metal or glass, used for the conveyance of fluids, and for various other purposes.
A vessel of animal bodies or plants, which conveys a fluid or other substance.
In botany, the narrow hollow part of a monopetalous corol, by which it is fixed to the receptacle.
In artillery, an instrument of tin, used in quick firing.
To furnish with a tube; as, to tube a well.
tuber: In botany, a knob in roots, solid, with the component particles all similar.
tubercle: A pimple; a small push, swelling or tumor on animal bodies.
A little knob, like a pimple, on plants; a little knob or rough point on the leaves of some lichens, supposed to be the fructification.
tuberculate: Having small knobs or pimples, as a plant.
tuberose: A plant with a tuberous root and a liliaceous flower, the Polianthus tuberosa; formerly called the tuberous hyacinth.
tuberous: Knobbed. In botany, consisting of roundish fleshy bodies, or tubers, connected into a bunch by intervening threads; as the roots of artichokes and potatoes.
tub-fish: A species of Trigla, sometimes called the flying-fish.
tubipore: A genus of zoophytes or corals.
tub-man: In the exchequer, a barrister so called.
tubular: Having the form of a tube or pipe; consisting of a pipe; fistular; as a tubular snout; a tubular calyx.
tubule: A small pipe or fistular body.
tubuliform: Having the form of a tube.
tubulous: Containing tubes; composed wholly of tubulous florets; as a tubulous compound flower.
In botany, having a bell-shaped border, with five reflex segments, rising from a tube; as a tubulous floret.
tuck: In a ship, the part where the ends of the bottom planks are collected under the stern.
A fold; a pull; a lugging.
To thrust or press in or together; to fold under; to press into a narrower compass; as, to tuck up a bed; to tuck up a garment; to tuck in the skirt of anything.
tucker: A small piece of linen for shading the breast of women.
tucket: A flourish in music; a voluntary; a prelude.
tucketsonance: The sound of the tucket, an ancient instrument of music.
tuesday: s as z. The third day of the week.
tufa: A stone or porous substance formed by depositions from springs or rivulets, containing much earthy matter in solution. Tufa is also formed by the concretion of loose volcanic dust or cinders, cemented by water, or by the consolidation of mud thrown out of volcanoes. The disintegration and subsequent consolidation of basaltic rocks, forms a kind of tufa, called by the German geologists, trap-tuff.
tuf: A stone or porous substance formed by depositions from springs or rivulets, containing much earthy matter in solution. Tufa is also formed by the concretion of loose volcanic dust or cinders, cemented by water, or by the consolidation of mud thrown out of volcanoes. The disintegration and subsequent consolidation of basaltic rocks, forms a kind of tufa, called by the German geologists, trap-tuff.
tufaceous: Pertaining to tufa; consisting of tufa, or resembling it.
tuffoon: A violent tempest or tornado with thunder and lightning, frequent in the Chinese sea and the gulf of Tonquin.
tuft: A collection of small things in a knot or bunch; as a tuft of flowers; a tuft of feathers; a tuft of grass or hair. A tuft of feathers forms the crest of a bird.
A cluster, a clump; as a tuft of trees; a tuft of olives.

In botany, a head of flowers, each elevated on a partial stalk, and all forming together a dense roundish mass. The word is sometimes applied to other collections, as little bundles of leaves, hairs and the like.
To adorn with tufts or with a tuft.
tufted: or a. Adorned with a tuft, as the tufted duck; growing in a tuft or clusters, as a tufted grove.
tufty: Abounding with tufts; growing in clusters; bushy.
tug: To pull or draw with great effort; to drag along with continued exertion; to haul along. There sweat, there strain, tug the laborious oar.
To pull; to pluck. --To ease the pain . His tugg’d ears suffer’d with a strain.
To pull with great effort; as, to tug at the oar; to tug against the stream.
To labor; to strive; to struggle. They long wrestled and strenuously tugged for their liberty.
A pull with the utmost effort. At the tug he falls-- . Vast ruins come along-- .
tugger: One who tugs, or pulls with great effort.
tugging: Pulling or dragging with great exertion; hauling.
tuition: Guardianship; superintending care over a young person; the particular watch and care of a tutor or guardian over his pupil or ward.
More especially, instruction; the act or business of teaching the various branches of learning. We place our children under the preceptors of academies for tuition.
The money paid for instruction. In our colleges, the tuition is from thirty to forty dollars a year.
tulip: A plant and a flower of the genus Tulipa, of a great variety of colors, and much cultivated for its beauty.
tulip-tree: An American tree bearing flowers resembling the tulip, of the genus Liriodendron. Also, a tree of the genus Magnolia.
tumble: To roll; to roll about by turning one way and the other; as, a person in pain tumbles and tosses.
To fall; to come down suddenly and violently; as, to tumble from a scaffold.
To roll down. The stone of Sisyphus is said to have tumbled to the bottom, as soon as it was carried up the hill.
To turn over; to turn or throw about for examination or searching; sometimes with over; as, to tumble over books or papers; to tumble over clothes.
tumbler: One who tumbles; one who plays the tricks of a mountebank.
A variety of the domestic pigeon, so called from his practice of tumbling or turning over in flight. It is a short-bodied pigeon, of a plain color, black, blue or white.
A sort of dog, so called form his practice of tumbling before he attacks his prey.
tumbling: Rolling about; falling; disturbing; rumpling. Tumbling-home, in a ship, is the inclination of the top-sides from a perpendicular, towards the center of the ship; or the part of a ship which falls inward above the extreme breadth.
tumbling-bay: In a canal, an overfall or weir.
tumbrel: A ducking stool for the punishment of scolds.2z
A cart or carriage with two wheels, which accompanies troops or artillery, for conveying the tools of pioneers, cartridges and the like.
tumbril: A contrivance of the basket kind, or a kind of cage of osiers, willowsfor keeping hay and other food for sheep.
tumefaction: The act or process of swelling or rising into a tumor; a tumor; a swelling.
tumefied: Swelled; enlarged; as a tumefied joint.
tumefy: To swell, or cause to swell.
To swell; to rise in a tumor.
tumid: Being swelled, enlarged or distended; as a tumid leg; tumid flesh.
Protuberant; rising above the level. So high as heav’d the tumid hills.
Swelling in sound or sense; pompous; puffy; bombastic; falsely sublime; as a tumid expression; a tumid style.
tumor: In surgery, a swelling; a morbid enlargement of any part of the body; a word of very comprehensive signification. The morbid enlargement of a particular part, without being caused by inflammation.
