Any swelling which arises from the growth of distinct superfluous parts or substances, which did not make any part of the original structure of the body, or from a morbid increase in the bulk of other parts, which naturally and always existed in the human frame. The term tumor is limited by Abernathy to such swellings as arise from new productions, and includes only the sarcomatous and encysted tumors. An encysted tumor is one which is formed in a membrane called a cyst, connected with the surrounding parts by the neighboring cellular substance. There are also fatty tumors, called lipomatous or adipose, (adipose sarcoma,) formed by an accumulation of fat in a limited extent of the cellular subs
Affected pomp; bombast in language; swelling word or expressions; false magnificence or sublimity.
tumorous: Vainly pompous; bombastic; as language or style.
tump: In gardening, to form a mass of earth or a hillock round a plant; as, to tump teasel.
tumped: Surrounded with a hillock of earth.
tumping: Raising a mass of earth round a plant.
tumular: Consisting in a heap; formed or being in a heap or hillock.
tumult: The commotion, disturbance or agitation of a multitude, usually accompanied with great noise, uproar and confusion of voices. What meaneth the noise of this tumult? 7 Samuel 4:74. Till in loud tumult all the Greeks arose.
Violent commotion or agitation with confusion of sounds; as the tumult of the elements.
Agitation; high excitement; irregular or confused motion; as the tumult of the spirits or passions.
To make a tumult; to be in great commotion.
tumultuarily: In a tumultuary or disorderly manner.
tumultuariness: Disorderly or tumultuous conduct; turbulence; disposition to tumult.
tumultuary: Disorderly; promiscuous; confused; as a tumultuary conflict.
Restless; agitated; unquiet. Men who live without religion, live always in a tumultuary and restless state.
tumultuation: Commotion; irregular or disorderly movement; as the tumultuation of the parts of a fluid.
tumultuous: Conducted with tumult; disorderly; as a tumultuous conflict; a tumultuous retreat.
Greatly agitated; irregular; noisy; confused; as a tumultuous assembly or meeting.
Agitated; disturbed; as a tumultuous breast.
Turbulent; violent; as a tumultuous speech.
Full of tumult and disorder; as a tumultuous state or city.
tumultuously: In a disorderly manner; by a disorderly multitude.
tumultuousness: The state of being tumultuous; disorder; commotion.
tun: In a general sense, a large cask; an oblong vessel bulging in the middle, like a pipe or puncheon, and girt with hoops.
A certain measure for liquids, as for wine, oil.
A quantity of wine, consisting of two pipes or four hogsheads, or 252 gallons. In different countries, the tun differs in quantity.

In commerce, the weight of twenty hundreds gross, each hundred consisting of 112 lb = 2240 Ib. But by a law of Connecticut, passed June 1827, gross weight is abolished, and a tun is the weight of 2000 Ib. It is also a practice in N. York to sell by 2000 Ib. to the tun.

A certain weight by which the burden of a ship is estimated; as a ship of three hundred tuns, that is, a ship that will carry three hundred times two thousand weight. Forty two cubic feet are allowed to a tun.
tunable: Harmonious; musical. And tunable as sylvan pipe or song.
That may be put in tune.
tune: A series of musical notes in some particular measure, and consisting of a single series, for one voice or instrument, the effect of which is melody; or a union of two or more series or parts to be sung or played in concert, the effect of which is harmony. Thus we say, a merry tune, a lively tune, a grave tune, a psalm tune, a martial tune.
Harmony; order; concert of parts. A continual parliament I thought would but keep the commonweal in tune.

The state of giving the proper sounds; as when we Say, a harpsichord is in tune; that is, when the several chords are of that tension, that each gives its proper sound, and the sounds of all are at due intervals, both of tones and semitones.

Proper state for use or application; right disposition; fit temper or humor. The mind is not in tune for mirth. A child will learn three times as fast when he is in tune, as he will when he is dragged to this task.
tuned: Uttered melodiously or harmoniously; put in order to produce the proper sounds.
tuneful: Harmonious; melodious; musical; as tuneful notes; tuneful birds.
tuneless: Not employed in making music; as a tuneless harp.
tuner: One whose occupation is to tune musical instruments.
tungstate: A salt formed of tungstenic acid and a base.
tungsten: In mineralogy, a mineral of a yellowish or grayish white color, of a lamellar structure, and infusible by the blowpipe. It occurs massive or crystallized, usually in octahedral crystals. This is an ore. The same name is given to the metal obtained from this ore. This metal is procured in small panes as fine as sand, of a strong metallic luster, an iron gray color, and slightly agglutinated. It is one of the hardest of the metals, and very brittle.
tungstenic: Pertaining to or procured from tungsten.
tunic: A kind of waistcoat or under garment worn by men in ancient Rome and the east. In the later ages of the republic, the tunic was a long garment with sleeves.
Among the religious, a woolen shirt or under garment.
In anatomy, a membrane that covers or composes some part or organ; as the tunics or coats of the eye; the tunics of the stomach, or the membranous and muscular layers which compose it.

A natural covering; an integument; as the tunic of a seed. The tunic of the seed, is the arillus, a covering attached to the base only of the seed, near the hilum or scar, and enveloping the rest of the seed more or less completely and closely.
tunicated: In botany, covered with a tunic or membranes; coated; as a stem. A tunicated bulb, is one composed of numerous concentric coats, as an onion.
tuning: Uttering harmoniously or melodiously; putting in due order for making the proper sounds.
tuning-fork: A steel instrument consisting of two prongs and a handle; used for tuning instruments.
tuning-hammer: An instrument for tuning instruments of music.
tunker: The tunkers are a religious sect in Pennsylvania, of German origin, resembling English baptists.
tunnage: The amount of tuns that a ship will carry; the content or burthen of a ship. A ship pays duty according to her tunnage.
The duty charged on ships according to their burthen, or the number of tuns at which they are rated.
A duty laid on liquors according to their measure.
A duty paid to mariners by merchants for unloading their ships, after a rate by the tun.
The whole amount of shipping, estimated by the tuns.
tunnel: A vessel with a broad mouth at one end, and a pipe or tube at the other, for conveying liquor into casks.
The opening of a chimney for the passage of smoke; called generally a funnel.
A large subterraneous arch through a hill for a canal and the passage of boats. Smaller drains or culverts are also called tunnels.
To form like a tunnel; as, to tunnel fibrous plants into nests.
To catch in a net called a tunnel-net.
tunnel-kiln: A lime-kiln in which coal is burnt, as distinguished from a flame kiln, in which wood or peat is used.
tunnel-net: A net with a wide mouth at one end and narrow at the other.
tunnel-pit: A shaft sunk from the top of the ground to the level of an intended tunnel, for drawing up the earth and stones.
tunny: A fish of the genus Scomber, the Spanish mackerel. The largest weigh upwards of four hundred pounds.
tupelo: A tree of the genus Nyssa.
tup-man: A man who deals in tups.
turban: A head dress worn by the orientals, consisting of a cap, and a sash of fine linen or taffeta artfully wound round it in plaits. The cap is red or green, roundish on the top, and quilted with cotton. The sash of the Turks is white linen; that of the Persians is red woolen.
In conchology, the whole set of whirls of a shell.
turbaned: Wearing a turban; as a turbaned Turk.
turban-shell: In natural history, a genus of shells, or rather of sea urchins, (echinodermata,) of a hemispheric or spheroidal shape, the Cidaris of Klein.
turban-top: A plant of the genus Helvella; a kind of fungus or mushroom.
turbary: In law, a right of digging turf on another man’s land. Common of turbary, is the liberty which a tenant enjoys of digging turf on the lord’s waste.
The place where turf is dug.
turbid: Properly, having the lees disturbed; but in a more general sense, muddy; foul with extraneous matter; thick; not clear; used of liquids of any kind; as turbid water; turbid wine. Streams running on clay generally appear to be turbid. This is often the case with the river Seine.
turbinate: In conchology, spiral, or wreathed conically from a larger base to a kind of apex; as turbinated shells.
In botany, shaped like a top or cone inverted; narrow at the base, and broad at the apex; as a turbinated germ, nectary or pericarp.
turbinated: In conchology, spiral, or wreathed conically from a larger base to a kind of apex; as turbinated shells.
In botany, shaped like a top or cone inverted; narrow at the base, and broad at the apex; as a turbinated germ, nectary or pericarp.
turbination: The act of spinning or whirling, as a top.
turbinite: A petrified shell of the turbo kind.
turbite: A petrified shell of the turbo kind.
turbit: A variety of the domestic pigeon, remarkable for its short beak; called by the Dutch kort-bek, short beak.
turbith: A root brought from the East Indies, particularly from Cambaya, Surat and Goa, or from Ceylon. It is the cortical part of the root of a species of Convolvulus. That sold in the shops is a longish root, of the thickness of the finger, resinous, heavy, and of a brownish hue without, but whitish within. It is cathartic. Turbith or turpeth mineral, is the yellow precipitate of mercury, called sometimes yellow subsulphate of mercury, or subdeutosulphate.
turpeth: A root brought from the East Indies, particularly from Cambaya, Surat and Goa, or from Ceylon. It is the cortical part of the root of a species of Convolvulus. That sold in the shops is a longish root, of the thickness of the finger, resinous, heavy, and of a brownish hue without, but whitish within. It is cathartic. Turbith or turpeth mineral, is the yellow precipitate of mercury, called sometimes yellow subsulphate of mercury, or subdeutosulphate.
turbot: A fish of the genus Pleuronectes, It grows to the weight of twenty or thirty pounds, and is much esteemed by epicures.
turbulence: A disturbed state; tumult; confusion; as the turbulence of the times; turbulence in political affairs.
Disorder or tumult of the passions; as turbulence of mind.
Agitation; tumultuousness; as turbulence of blood.
Disposition to resist authority; insubordination; as the turbulence of subjects.
turbulency: A disturbed state; tumult; confusion; as the turbulence of the times; turbulence in political affairs.
Disorder or tumult of the passions; as turbulence of mind.
Agitation; tumultuousness; as turbulence of blood.
Disposition to resist authority; insubordination; as the turbulence of subjects.
turbulent: Disturbed; agitated; tumultuous; being in violent commotion; as the turbulent ocean. Calm region once, And full of peace, now tost and turbulent. The turbulent mirth of wine.
Restless; unquiet; refractory; disposed to insubordination and disorder; as turbulent spirits.
Producing commotion. Whose heads that turbulent liquor fills with fumes.
turbulently: Tumultuously; with violent agitation; with refractoriness.
turf: That upper stratum of earth and vegetable mold, which is filled with the roots of grass and other small plants, so as to adhere and form a kind of mat. This is otherwise called sward and sod.
Peat; a peculiar kind of blackish, fibrous, vegetable, earthy substance, used as fuel.
Race-ground; or horse-racing. The honors of the turf are all our own.
To cover with turf or sod; as, to turf a bank or the border of a terrace.
turf-drain: A drain filled with turf or peat.
turfed: Covered with turf or green sod.
turf-hedge: A hedge or fence formed with turf and plants of different kinds.
turf-house: A house or shed formed of turf, common in the northern parts of Europe.
turfiness: The state of abounding with turf, or of having the consistence or qualities of turf.
turfing: The operation of laying down turf, or covering with turf.
turfing-iron: An implement for paring off turf.
turfing-spade: An instrument for under-cutting turf, when marked out by the plow.
turf-moss: A tract of turfy, mossy, or boggy land.
turf-spade: A spade for cutting and digging turf, longer and narrower than the common spade.
turfy: Having the qualities of turf. TURF Y.
turgent: Swelling; tumid; rising into a tumor or puffy state; as when the humors are turgent.
turgid: Swelled; bloated; distended beyond its natural state by some internal agent or expansive force. A bladder held by the fire grew turgid. More generally, the word is applied to an enlarged part of the body; as a turgid limb.
Tumid; pompous; inflated; bombastic; as a turgid style; a turgid manner of talking.
turgidness: A swelling or swelled state of a thing; distention beyond its natural state by some internal force or agent, as in a limb.
Pompousness; inflated manner of writing or speaking; bombast; as the turgidness of language or style.
turkey: A large fowl, the Meleagris gallopavo, a distinct genus. It is a native of America, and its flesh furnishes most delicious food. Wild turkies abound in the forests of America, and domestic turkies are bred in other countries, as well as in America.
turky: A large fowl, the Meleagris gallopavo, a distinct genus. It is a native of America, and its flesh furnishes most delicious food. Wild turkies abound in the forests of America, and domestic turkies are bred in other countries, as well as in America.
turkey-stone: Another name of the oil-stone, from Turkey.
turkois: A mineral, called also calaite, brought from the east; of a beautiful light green color, occurring in thin layers, or in rounded masses, or in reniform masses, with a botryoidal surface. It is susceptible of a high polish, and is used in jewelry. It is usually written in the French manner.
turmeric: Indian saffron; a medicinal root brought from the East Indies, the root of the Curcuma longa. It is externally grayish, but internally of a deep lively yellow or saffron color. It has a slight aromatic smell, and a bitterish, slightly acrid taste. It is used for dyeing, and in some cases, as a medicine. This name is sometimes given to the blood- root of America.
turmoil: Disturbance, tumult; harassing labor; trouble; molestation by tumult. There I'll rest, as after much turmoil . A blessed soul doth in Elysium.
To harass with commotion. It is her fatal misfortune--to be miserably tossed and turmoiled with these storms of affliction.
To be disquieted; to be in commotion.
turn: To cause to move in a circular course; as, to turn a wheel; to turn a spindle; to turn the body.
To change or shift sides; to put the upper side downwards, or one side in the place of the other. It is said a hen turns her eggs often when sitting.
To alter, as a position. Expert . When to advance, or stand, or turn the sway of battle.
To cause to preponderate; to change the state of a balance; as, to turn the scale.
turncoat: One who forsakes his party or principles.
turnep: A bulbous root or plant of the genus Brassica, of great value for food; an esculent root of several varieties.
turner: One whose occupation is to form things with a lathe; one who turns.
turnerite: A rare mineral occurring in small crystals of a yellowish brown color, externally brilliant and translucent.
turnery: The art of forming into a cylindrical shape by the lathe.
Things made by a turner or in the lathe.
turning: Moving in a circle; changing; winding.
A winding; a bending course; flexure; meander.
Deviation from the way or proper course.
turnpike: Strictly, a frame consisting of two bars crossing each other at right angles, and turning on a post or pin, to hinder the passage of breasts, but admitting a person to pass between the arms.
A gate set across a road to stop travelers and carriages till toll is paid for keeping the road in repair.
In military affairs, a beam filled with spikes to obstruct passage.
To form, as a road, in the manner of a turnpike road; to throw the path of a road into a rounded form.
turnpike-road: A road on which turnpikes or toll-gates are established by law, and which are made and kept in repair by the toll collected from travelers or passengers who use the road.
turnserving: The act or practice of serving one’s turn or promoting private interest.
turnsole: A plant of the genus Heliotropium, so named because its flower is supposed to turn towards the sun.
turnspit: A person who turns a spit. His lordship is his majesty’s turnspit.
A variety of the dog, so called from turning the spit.
turnstone: A bird, called the sea-dotterel, the Tringa morinella, a little larger than an English blackbird. This bird takes its name from its practice of turning up small stones in search of insects.
turpentine: A transparent resinous substance, flowing naturally or by incision from several species of trees, as from the pine, larch, firCommon turpentine is of about the consistence of honey; but there are several varieties.
turpentine-tree: A tree of the genus Pistacia, which produces not only its proper fruit, but a kind of horn which grows on the surface of its leaves. This is found to be an excrescence, the effect of the puncture of an insect, and is produced in the same manner as the galls of other plants.
turpitude: Inherent baseness or vileness of principle in the human heart; extreme depravity.
Baseness or vileness of words or actions; shameful wickedness.
turret: A little tower; a small eminence or spire attached to a building and rising above it. And lift her turrets nearer to the sky.

In the art of war, movable turrets, used formerly by the Romans, were buildings of a square form, consisting of ten or even twenty stories, and sometimes one hundred and twenty cubits high, moved on wheels. They were employed in approaches to a fortified place, for carrying soldiers, engines, ladders, casting-bridges and other necessaries.
turreted: Formed like a tower; as a turreted lamp.
turrilite: The fossil remains of a spiral multilocular shell.
turtle: A fowl of the genus Columba; called also the turtle dove, and turtle pigeon. It is a wild species, frequenting the thickest parts of the woods, and its note is plaintive and tender.
The name sometimes given to the common tortoise.
The name given to the large sea-tortoise.
turtle-dove: A species of the genus Columba.
turtle-shell: A shell, a beautiful species of Murex; also, tortoise- shell.
tuscan: Pertaining to Tuscany in Italy; an epithet given to one of the orders of columns, the most ancient and simple.
tush: an exclamation, indicating check, rebuke or contempt. Tush, tush, never tell me such a story as that.
tusk: The long pointed tooth of certain rapacious, carnivorous or fighting animals; as the tusks of the boar.
To gnash the teeth, as a boar.
tusked: Furnished with tusks; as the tusky boar.
tusky: Furnished with tusks; as the tusky boar.
tussuc: A tuft of grass or twigs.
tussoc: A tuft of grass or twigs.
tut: an exclamation, used for checking or rebuking.
An imperial ensign of a golden globe with a cross on it. Tut bargain, among miners, a bargain by the lump.
tutelage: Guardianship; protection; applied to the person protecting; as, the king’s right of seignory and tutelage.
State of being under a guardian.
tutelar: Having the guardianship or charge of protecting a person or a thing; guardian; protecting; as tutelary genii; tutelary goddesses.
tutelary: Having the guardianship or charge of protecting a person or a thing; guardian; protecting; as tutelary genii; tutelary goddesses.
tutenag: The Chinese name of zink. Sometimes the word is used to denote a metallic compound brought from China, called Chinese copper or white copper, consisting of copper, zink and iron.
tutor: In the civil law, a guardian; one who has the charge of a child or pupil and his estate.
One who has the care of instructing another in various branches or in any branch of human learning. Some gentlemen employ a tutor to teach in their families, others to attend a son in his travels.

In universities and colleges, an officer or member of some hall, who has the charge of instructing the students in the sciences and other branches of learning. In the American colleges, tutors are graduates selected by the governors or trustees, for the instruction of undergraduates of the three first years. They are usually officers of the institution, who have a share, with the president and professors, in the government of the students.
To treat with authority or severity.
tutorage: In the civil law, guardianship; the charge of a pupil and his estate. In France, tutorage does not expire till the pupil is twenty five years of age.
The authority or solemnity of a tutor.
tutoress: A female tutor; an instructress; a governess.
tutsan: A plant, park-leaves, of the genus Hypericum.
tutti: In Italian music, a direction for all to play in full concert.
tutty: An argillaceous ore of zink, found in Persia, formed on cylindric molds into tubular pieces, like the bark of a tree. It is said to be made of a glutinous, argillaceous earth, like clay, which is put into pots, moistened and baked.
tuz: A lock or tuft of hair.
twain: or n. Two. When old winter splits the rocks in twain.
twait: In old writers, wood grubbed up and converted into arable land.
twang: To sound with a quick sharp noise; to make the sound of a string which is stretched and suddenly pulled; as the twanging bows.
To make to sound, as by pulling a tense string and letting it go suddenly. Sound the tough horn, and twang the quivering string.
A sharp quick sound; as the twang of a bowstring; a twang of the nose.
An affected modulation of the voice; a kind of nasal sound. He has a twang in his discourse.
twattle: To prate; to talk much and idly; to gabble; to chatter; as a twattling gossip.
To pet; to make much of.
twattling: The act of prating; idle talk.
tway-blade: A plant of the genus Ophris; a polypetalous flower, consisting of six dissimilar leaves, of which the five upper ones are so disposed as to represent, in some measure, a helmet, the under one being headed and shaped like a man.
twy-blade: A plant of the genus Ophris; a polypetalous flower, consisting of six dissimilar leaves, of which the five upper ones are so disposed as to represent, in some measure, a helmet, the under one being headed and shaped like a man.
tweag: It is radically the same word as twitch, and of the same signification. To twitch; to pinch and pull with a sudden jerk; as, to tweag or tweak the nose.
tweak: It is radically the same word as twitch, and of the same signification. To twitch; to pinch and pull with a sudden jerk; as, to tweag or tweak the nose.
tweedle: To handle lightly; used of awkward fiddling.
tweel: To weave with multiplied leases in the harness, by increasing the number of threads in each split of the reed, and the number of treddles
tweezers: Nippers; small pinchers used to pluck out hairs.
twelfth: The second after the tenth; the ordinal of twelve.
twelve: The sum of two and ten; twice six; a dozen. Twelve men compose a petty jury.
twelvemonth: twelv’month. A year, which consists of twelve calendar months. I shall laugh at this a twelvemonth hence.
twelvepenny: twelv’penny. Sold for a shilling; worth a shilling; as a twelve-penny gallery.
twelvescore: twelv’score. Twelve times twenty; two hundred and forty.
twentieth: The ordinal of twenty; as the twentieth year.
twenty: Twice ten; as twenty men; twenty years.
Proverbially, an indefinite number. Maximilian, upon twenty respects, could not have been the man.
twibil: A kind of mattock, and a halbert.
twice: Two times. He twice essay’d to cast his son in gold.
Doubly; as twice the sum. He is twice as fortunate as his neighbor.
Twice is used in composition; as in twice-told, twice-born, twice- planted, twice-conquered.
twifallow: To plow a second time land that is fallowed.
twifallowing: The operation of plowing a second time, as fallow land, in preparing it for seed.
twig: A small shoot or branch of a tree or other plant, of no definite length or size. The Britons had boats made of willow twigs, covered on the outside with hides.
twiggy: Full of twigs; abounding with shoots.
twilight: The faint light which is reflected upon the earth after sunset and before sunrise; crepuscular light. In latitudes remote from the equator, the twilight is of much longer duration than at and near the equator.
Dubious or uncertain view; as the twilight or probability.
Obscure; imperfectly illuminated; shaded. O’er the twilight groves and dusky caves.
twill: To weave in ribs or ridges; to quill.
twin: One of two young produced at a birth by an animal that ordinarily brings but one; used mostly in the plural, twins; applied to the young of beasts, as well as to human beings.
A sign of the zodiac; Gemini.
Noting one of two born at a birth; as a twin brother or sister.
twine: To twist; to wind, as one thread or cord around another, or as any flexible substance around another, or as any flexible substance around another body; as fine twined linen. Exodus 39:2.
To unite closely; to cling to; to embrace.
To gird; to wrap closely about. Let wreaths of triumph now my temples twine.
To unite closely, or by interposition of parts. Friends now fast sworn, who twine in love-- .
To wind; to bend; to make turns. As rivers, though they bend and twine-- .
twinge: To affect with a sharp sudden pain; to torment with pinching or sharp pains. The gnat twinged the lion till he made him tear himself, and so he mastered him.
To pinch; to tweak; to pull with a jerk; as, to twinge one by the ears and nose.
twinj. to have a sudden, sharp, local pain, like a twitch; to suffer a keen spasmodic or shooting pain; as, the side twinges.
A sudden sharp pain; a darting local pain of momentary continuance; as a twinge in the arm or side.
twinging: Suffering a sharp local pain of short continuance; pinching with a sudden pull.
The act of pinching with a sudden twitch; a sudden, sharp, local pain.
twining: Twisting; winding round; uniting closely to; embracing.
In botany, ascending spirally around a branch, stem or prop.
twinkle: To sparkle; to flash at intervals; to shine with a tremulous intermitted light, or with a broken quivering light. The fixed stars twinkle; the planets do not. These stars do not twinkle, when viewed through telescopes that have large apertures.
To open and shut the eye by turns; as the twinkling owl.
To play irregularly; as, her eyes will twinkle.
A sparkling; a shining with intermitted light; as the twinkling of the stars.
twinkling: A sparkling; a shining with intermitted light; as the twinkling of the stars.
Amoment; an instant; the time of a wink. In a moment, in the twinkling of an eye, at the last trump--the dead shall be raised incorruptible. 7 Corinthians 15:52.
twinned: Produced at one birth, like twins; united.
twire: To take short flights; to flutter; to quiver; to twitter.
twirl: twurl. To move or turn round with rapidity; to whirl round. See ruddy maids, Some taught with dextrous hand to twirl the wheel-- .
To revolve with velocity; to be whirled round.
A rapid circular motion; quick rotation.
twist: To unite by winding one thread, strand or other flexible substance round another; to form by convolution, or winding separate things round each other; as to twist yarn or thread. So we say, to double and twist.
To form into a thread from many fine filaments; as, to twist wool or cotton.
To contort; to writhe; as, to twist a thing into a serpentine form.
To wreathe; to wind; to encircle. --Pillars of smoke twisted about with wreaths of flame.
To form; to weave; as, to twist a story.
twisted: Formed by winding threads or strands round each other.
twisting: Winding different strands or threads round each other; forming into a thread by twisting.
twit: To reproach; to upbraid; as for some previous act. He twitted his friend of falsehood. With this these scoffers twitted the christians. Aesop minds men of their errors, without twitting them for what is amiss.
twitch: é To pull with a sudden jerk; to pluck with a short, quick motion; to snatch; as, to twitch one by the sleeve; to twitch a thing out of another’s hand; to twitch off clusters of grapes.
A pull with a jerk; a short, sudden, quick pull; as a twitch by the sleeve.
A short spasmodic contraction of the fibers or muscles; as a twitch in the side; convulsive twitches.
twitch-grass: Couch grass; a species of grass which it is difficult to exterminate. But qu. is not this word a corruption of quitch-grass, or quick-grass?
twitching: Pulling with a jerk; suffering short spasmodic contractions.
twitter: To make a succession of small, tremulous, intermitted noises; as, the swallow twitters.
To make the sound of a half suppressed laugh.
A small intermitted noise, as in half suppressed laughter; or the sound of a swallow.
twittering: Uttering a succession of small interrupted sounds, as in a half suppressed laugh, or as a swallow.
twixt: a contraction of betwixt, used in poetry.
two: One and one. Two similar horses used together, are called a span, or a pair.
Two is used in composition; as in two-legged. Man is a two- legged animal.
twoedged: Having two edges, or edges on both sides; as a two-edged sword.
two-flowered: Bearing two flowers at the end, as a peduncle.
twofold: Doubly; in a double degree. Matthew 23:15.
Two of the same kind, or two different things existing together; as twofold nature; a twofold sense; a twofold argument.
Double; as twofold strength or desire. T IOFOLD.
In botany, two and two together, growing form the same place; as twofold leaves.
two-handed: Having two hands; an epithet used as equivalent to large, stout and strong.
two-seeded: In botany, dispermous; containing two seeds, as a fruit; having two seeds to a flower, as a plant.
two-valved: Bivalvular, as a shell, pod, or glume.
tyke: A dog; or one as contemptible as a dog.
tymbal: A kind of kettle drum. A tymbal’s sound were better than my voice.
tympan: A drum; hence, the barrel or hollow part of the ear behind the membrane of the tympanum.
The area of a pediment; also, the part of a pedestal called the trunk or dye.
A triangular space or table in the corners or sides of an arch, usually enriched with figures.
Among printers, a frame covered with parchment or cloth, on which the blank sheets are put in order to be laid on the form to be impressed.
tympanites: In medicine, a flatulent distention of the belly; wind dropsy; tympany.
tympanize: To act the part of a drummer.
To stretch, as a skin over the head of a drum.
tympanum: In mechanics, a wheel placed round an axis.
tympany: A flatulent distention of the belly.
type: The mark of something; an emblem; that which represents something else. Thy emblem, gracious queen, the British rose, Type of sweet rule and gentle majesty.
A sign; a symbol; a figure of something to come; as, Abraham’s sacrifice and the paschal lamb, were types of Christ. To this word is opposed antitype. Christ, in this case, is the antitype.
To prefigure; to represent by a model or symbol beforehand.
type-metal: A compound of lead and antimony, with a small quantity of copper or U is the twenty first letter and the fifth vowel in the English Alphabet. The sound seems to be nearly that of eu, shortened and blended. This sound however is not precisely that of eu or yu, except in a few words, as in unite, union, uniform; the sound does not begin with the distinct sound of e, nor end in the distinct sound of 00, unless when prolonged. It cannot be well expressed in letters. This sound is heard in the unaffected pronunciation of annuity, numerate, brute, mute, dispute, duke, true, truth, rule, prudence, opportunity
A compound of lead and antimony, with a small quantity of copper or brass.
tfaiqe: The seed of pulpy fruit; as the kernel of an apple.
thermolanp: An instrument for furnishing light by means of inflammable gas. THERMOLAMP.
tinder-box: A box in which tinder is kept.
trangranm: An odd thing intricately contrived. Trannel, used by Moxon, is a mistake for tree-nail, pronounced by ship-builders trunnel. TRANGRAM.
transfkx: To pierce through, as with a pointed weapon; as, to transfix one with a dart or spear. TRANSFIX.
trencher-fly: One that haunts the tables of others; a parasite.
tfrip: Among farmers, a small flock of sheep, or a small stock of them. TRIP.
types: A model or form of a letter in metal or other hard material; used in printing. TYPE.
In medicine, the form or character of a disease, in regard to the intension and remission of fevers, pulses; the regular progress of a fever. TYPE.
In natural history, a general form, such as is common to the species of a genus, or the individuals of a species. TYPE.
typhus: A typhus disease or fever is accompanied with great debility. The word is sometimes used as a noun.
typic: Emblematic; figurative; representing something future by a form, model or resemblance. Abraham’s offering of his only son Isaac, was typical of the sacrifice of Christ. The brazen serpent was typical of the cross. Typic fever, is one that is regular in its attacks; opposed to erratic fever.
typical: Emblematic; figurative; representing something future by a form, model or resemblance. Abraham’s offering of his only son Isaac, was typical of the sacrifice of Christ. The brazen serpent was typical of the cross. Typic fever, is one that is regular in its attacks; opposed to erratic fever.
typically: In a typical manner; by way of image, symbol or resemblance.
typify: To represent by an image, form, model or resemblance. The washing of baptism typifies the cleansing of the soul from sin by the blood of Christ. Our Savior was typified by the goat that was slain.
typographic: Pertaining to printing; as the typographic art.
typographical: Pertaining to printing; as the typographic art.
typographically: By means of types; after the manner of printers.
typography: The art of printing, or the operation of impressing letters and words on forms of types.
typolite: In natural history, a stone or fossil which has on it impressions or figures of plants and animals.
tyrannic: Pertaining to a tyrant; suiting a tyrant; arbitrary; unjustly severe in government; imperious; despotic; cruel; as a tyrannical prince; a tyrannical master; tyrannical government or power. Our sects a more tyrannic power assume. Th’ oppressor rul’d tyrannic where he durst.
tyrannical: Pertaining to a tyrant; suiting a tyrant; arbitrary; unjustly severe in government; imperious; despotic; cruel; as a tyrannical prince; a tyrannical master; tyrannical government or power. Our sects a more tyrannic power assume. Th’ oppressor rul’d tyrannic where he durst.
tyrannically: With unjust exercise of power; arbitrarily; oppressively.
tyrannicide: The act of killing a tyrant.
tyranning: or a. Acting as a tyrant.
tyrannize: To act the tyrant; to exercise arbitrary power; to rule with unjust and oppressive severity; to exercise power over others not permitted by law or required by justice, or with a severity not necessary to the ends of justice and government. A prince will often tyrannize over his subjects; republican legislatures sometimes tyrannize over their fellow citizens, masters sometimes tyrannize over their servants or apprentices. A husband may not tyrannize over his wife and children.
tyranny: Arbitrary or despotic exercise of power; the exercise of power over subjects and others with a rigor not authorized by law or justice, or not requisite for the purposes of government. Hence tyranny is often synonymous with cruelty and oppression./\V/ .
Cruel government or discipline; as the tyranny of a master.
Severity; rigor; inclemency. The tyranny o’ th’ open night.
tyrant: U U is the twenty first letter and the fifth vowel in the English Alphabet. The sound seems to be nearly that of eu, shortened and blended. This sound however is not precisely that of eu or yu, except in a few words, as in unite, union, uniform; the sound does not begin with the distinct sound of e, nor end in the distinct sound of oo, unless when prolonged. It cannot be well expressed in letters. This sound is heard in the unaffected pronunciation of annuity, numerate, brute, mute, dispute, duke, true, truth, rule, prudence, opportunitySome modern writers make a distinction between the sound of u, when it follows r, as in rude, truth, and its sound when it follows other letters, as in mute, duke; making the former sound equivalent to 00; rood, trooth; and the latter a diphthong equivalent to eu or yu.
